item.title: Venerable Jatukaṇṇī: Having heard of the hero not attached to sensual pleasures, I have come here to ask the desireless one Who has crossed the…
item.description:
Venerable Jatukaṇṇī:
Having heard of the hero not attached to sensual pleasures,
I have come here to ask the desireless one
Who has crossed the flood, born with the (wisdom-)eye.
Please tell the path of peace — let the Blessed One truly explain it to me.

Having conquered sensual desires, the Blessed One dwells in the world,
Like the sun illuminating the earth with radiance.
O one of vast wisdom, teach me the Dhamma,
So that I, of little wisdom, may know it
And here abandon birth and old age.

The Blessed One replied to Jatukaṇṇī:
Subdue greed for sensual pleasures;
See renunciation as security.
Do not grasp or abandon anything in the world.

Dry up what is before you; have nothing after.
If you do not grasp in the middle, you will live peacefully.

O brahmin, for one completely without greed for name-and-form,
Without taints — because of these taints one is overcome by death.

From: Sutta Nipāta 5.12
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: About the downfall of a person, I ask you, Gotama. Having come to ask the Blessed One, tell me the cause of downfall. It is…
item.description:
About the downfall of a person, I ask you, Gotama.
Having come to ask the Blessed One, tell me the cause of downfall.

It is easy to know a prosperous person, easy to know one who is down.
One who loves the Dhamma prospers; one who hates the Dhamma declines.

One who loves the bad and not the good,
Who approves of the bad — that is the cause of downfall.

One who is fond of sleep, fond of company, lazy, of little energy,
Given to anger — that is the cause of downfall.

From: Sutta Nipāta 1.6
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Khandasumana: Having given up one flower, I enjoyed eighty thousand years in heaven, And the remaining five years I have lived at peace.
item.description:
Elder Khandasumana:

Having given up one flower, I enjoyed eighty thousand years in heaven,
And the remaining five years I have lived at peace.

From: Theragāthā 1.96
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Friends, what is the development of concentration that, when developed and frequently practiced, leads to mindfulness and clear comprehension? Friends, here, a bhikkhu knows the…
item.description:
Friends, what is the development of concentration that, when developed and frequently practiced, leads to mindfulness and clear comprehension? Friends, here, a bhikkhu knows the arising of feelings, knows their persisting, knows their passing away; knows the arising of perceptions, knows their persisting, knows their passing away; knows the arising of thoughts, knows their persisting, knows their passing away. Friends, this is the development of concentration that, when developed and frequently practiced, leads to mindfulness and clear comprehension.

From: Dīgha Nikāya 33 – Saṅgītisutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: So too, bhikkhus, in the future, there will be bhikkhus. Those suttas spoken by the Tathāgata, profound, deeply meaningful, transcendent, dealing with emptiness, they will…
item.description:
So too, bhikkhus, in the future, there will be bhikkhus. Those suttas spoken by the Tathāgata, profound, deeply meaningful, transcendent, dealing with emptiness, they will not listen to when they are being taught; they will not lend an ear; they will not apply their minds to true knowledge; and they will not think that those teachings are worth learning and mastering. But those suttas that are composed by poets, beautiful verses with beautiful phrases, belonging to outsiders, spoken by disciples, they will listen to when they are being taught; they will lend an ear; they will apply their minds to true knowledge; and they will think that those teachings are worth learning and mastering. Thus, bhikkhus, those suttas spoken by the Tathāgata, profound, deeply meaningful, transcendent, dealing with emptiness, will disappear. Therefore, bhikkhus, you should train thus: 'Those suttas spoken by the Tathāgata, profound, deeply meaningful, transcendent, dealing with emptiness — we will listen to when they are being taught; we will lend an ear; we will apply our minds to true knowledge; and we will think that those teachings are worth learning and mastering.' Thus, bhikkhus, you should train.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 20.7
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The Blessed One replied to Bhadrāvudha: Subdue all grasping and craving Above, below, across, and in between. Whoever grasps anything in the world, Because of…
item.description:
The Blessed One replied to Bhadrāvudha:
Subdue all grasping and craving
Above, below, across, and in between.
Whoever grasps anything in the world,
Because of that, Māra follows that person.

Therefore, a wise person, not grasping anything,
A bhikkhu mindful in the entire world,
Having no possessions, does not long.
Behold these people who grasp,
In Māra's domain, attached and clinging.

From: Sutta Nipāta 5.13
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - What do you think, bhikkhus, which is greater: the few Simsapā leaves I have taken in my hand, or the leaves in the Simsapā…
item.description:
- What do you think, bhikkhus, which is greater: the few Simsapā leaves I have taken in my hand, or the leaves in the Simsapā forest?" - "Venerable Sir, the few Simsapā leaves the Blessed One has taken in his hand are very few, and the leaves in the Simsapā forest are far more numerous." - "So too, bhikkhus, what I have directly known but not taught to you is far more numerous; what I have taught you is very little. But why, bhikkhus, have I not taught those things? Because they are not connected with the goal, not fundamental to the holy life, and do not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna. Therefore, I have not taught them. And what, bhikkhus, have I taught? 'This is suffering,' bhikkhus, I have taught. 'This is the origin of suffering,' I have taught. 'This is the cessation of suffering,' I have taught. 'This is the path leading to the cessation of suffering,' I have taught. And why, bhikkhus, have I taught these things? Because they are connected with the goal, fundamental to the holy life, and lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna. Therefore, I have taught them.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 56.31
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: There are these two things, bhikkhus, that are components of true knowledge. What two? Serenity and insight. When serenity is developed, bhikkhus, what benefit is…
item.description:
There are these two things, bhikkhus, that are components of true knowledge. What two? Serenity and insight. When serenity is developed, bhikkhus, what benefit is to be expected? The mind is developed. When the mind is developed, what benefit is to be expected? Greed is abandoned. When insight is developed, bhikkhus, what benefit is to be expected? Wisdom is developed. When wisdom is developed, what benefit is to be expected? Ignorance is abandoned. Defiled by greed, bhikkhus, the mind is not liberated. Defiled by ignorance, wisdom is not developed. Therefore, through the removal of greed, there is liberation of mind; through the removal of ignorance, there is liberation by wisdom.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 2.31
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Master Gotama, just as among root fragrances, black sandalwood is considered supreme; among heartwood fragrances, red sandalwood is considered supreme; among flower fragrances, jasmine is…
item.description:
Master Gotama, just as among root fragrances, black sandalwood is considered supreme; among heartwood fragrances, red sandalwood is considered supreme; among flower fragrances, jasmine is considered supreme. So too, the instruction of Master Gotama is considered supreme among instructions today.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 107 – Gaṇakamoggallānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Nun Mother of Vaḍḍha: Vaḍḍha, never fall Into the realm of sensual desire, My son, do not share In continuous suffering. Happiness is for…
item.description:
Elder Nun Mother of Vaḍḍha:

Vaḍḍha, never fall
Into the realm of sensual desire,
My son, do not share
In continuous suffering.

Happiness is for the seer,
Without greed, doubt cut off,
Living cooled, subdued,
Without any taints.

That seer practices
The path to right view,
Ending suffering.
Vaḍḍha! Practice.

With confidence, the mother spoke
This goal for you.
Dear mother! I think that you
No longer have sensual desire.

Vaḍḍha! Whatever conduct — low, high, or middle —
Not even a little sensual desire is in me.

I have destroyed all taints,
Not heedless, meditating.
I have attained the three knowledges,
Done is the teaching.

Wonderful is my mother,
Encouraging, urging me,
With verses of truth,
Out of compassion.

I heard the mother's instruction,
The advice of my dear mother.
Moved, I practiced the Dhamma,
Attaining security from bondage.

I am energetic, diligent,
Day and night not lazy.
Well urged by my mother,
I attained supreme purity.

From: Therīgāthā 9.1
📖 Read this sutta
